title: Demo 7 — Mapping Color Patterns onto a Phylogeny
text:
Today’s big question

Do closely related fish species have similar color patterns?


Or does natural selection override evolutionary history?


We’ll answer this with data, a phylogeny, and two statistical tests.

title: Demo 7 — Mapping Color Patterns onto a Phylogeny
text:
What is a phylogeny?



A family tree for species
Branch lengths = time (millions of years)
Closely related species share a recent common ancestor
The tree is estimated from DNA sequences


Today’s tree: 65 surgeonfish species (Acanthuridae)
Root age: 65 million years — right at the end-Cretaceous extinction!


        ┌── Acanthurus
    ┌───┤
    │   └── Ctenochaetus
────┤
    │   ┌── Zebrasoma
    └───┤
        └── Naso

(simplified — the real tree has 65 tips)

title: Demo 7 — Mapping Color Patterns onto a Phylogeny
section: What are we mapping onto the tree?
text:
What are we mapping onto the tree?
Color pattern traits — coded by Frederich et al. for each species:


HEAD (H_)

Colors
Horizontal stripes
Vertical stripes
Eyespots
Marbling


TRUNK (Tr_)

Colors
Horizontal stripes
Vertical stripes
Spots/points
Marbling


TAIL (Ta_)

Colors
Stripes
Eyespots
Saddle marks
Colored scalpel



52 binary traits (present/absent) across 3 body regions = a detailed color pattern fingerprint for each species

title: Demo 7 — Mapping Color Patterns onto a Phylogeny
text:
What is phylogenetic signal?

The tendency for closely related species to resemble each other more than distantly related species.



Strong signal
Relatives look alike → color patterns evolve slowly

Pattern is “conserved” on the tree


Weak/no signal
Relatives look different → color changes fast, convergence is common

Pattern evolves independently of relatedness



We measure this with two statistics: Blomberg’s K and Pagel’s λ

title: Demo 7 — Mapping Color Patterns onto a Phylogeny
section: Blomberg’s K — what the number means
text:
Blomberg’s K — what the number means







K value
Interpretation




K = 0
No signal — trait is random on the tree


0 < K < 1
Signal exists, but weaker than expected under Brownian motion


K = 1
Trait matches Brownian motion prediction exactly


K > 1
Relatives are MORE similar than Brownian motion predicts




The p-value tells you if the signal is statistically significant:

p < 0.05 → yes, there’s real signal
p > 0.05 → can’t distinguish from random



K = 0.16 with p = 0.04 → “there IS signal, but it’s modest — color patterns show some phylogenetic conservatism but also a lot of independent change”

title: Demo 7 — Mapping Color Patterns onto a Phylogeny
section: contMap — painting traits on the tree
text:
contMap — painting traits on the tree
contMap reconstructs ancestral trait values and paints them along every branch.



Cool colors (blue) = simple patterns
Warm colors (red) = complex patterns
You can SEE where on the tree complexity changed
Look for abrupt shifts — those are evolutionary events



You’ll generate one of these in Section 5 of the worksheet.
The most complex species? Acanthurus bariene — 19 pattern elements!
The simplest? Zebrasoma rostratum — only 4 elements.

title: Demo 7 — Mapping Color Patterns onto a Phylogeny
text:
Discrete traits — mapping presence/absence
Not all traits are continuous. Some are binary: the species either has it or doesn’t.
Example: trunk marbling (irregular worm-like markings)



17 of 62 species have trunk marbling
Concentrated in Acanthurus (11/26) and Ctenochaetus (3/6)
Absent in Zebrasoma, Prionurus, Paracanthurus



Discussion question:
Is marbling a Turing pattern?
Think back to Lecture 12 — reaction-diffusion produces labyrinthine patterns that look a lot like “marbling.”



You’ll color tree tips by marbling presence and ask: how many times did it evolve?

text:
What you’ll turn in


Screenshot of your contMap (pattern complexity on the tree)
Your K and λ values with a one-sentence interpretation each
Your chosen trait from “Your Turn” — which trait did you pick? What was K? Was it significant?

There is no single “right answer” — we want a nuanced interpretation backed by your data.

Getting started


Open RStudio

Open the worksheet: demo7-worksheet.qmd (or the HTML version in your browser)
Work through it section by section — don’t skip ahead

EEB 187: Ecology and Evolution of Color Week 8, Wednesday May 20, 2026 Duration: 75 minutes




R packages installed on all lab machines: ape, phytools. Test with library(ape); library(phytools) — no errors.
Data files in place on all machines or on the shared drive. The folder structure should be:
demo7/
  data/
    frederich/
      acanthuridae-tree.tree    (NEXUS format, 65 tips)
      acanthuridae-color-patterns.csv   (83 species, 59 columns)
  demo7-worksheet.qmd
Test-render the QMD on at least one machine: quarto render demo7-worksheet.qmd --to html. Confirm that all chunks execute, especially the contMap and phylosig calls (these take a few seconds each).
Print 5–6 paper copies of the QMD (or have the HTML open on the podium) for students who prefer to follow along on paper.
Projector works with RStudio visible. You will want to live-code Sections 1–3 while students follow along.
setwd() paths: Students will need to change the setwd() path. Have the correct path for the lab machines written on the board or in a pinned Slack message before class starts. Something like: setwd("C:/Users/student/Desktop/demo7/") or setwd("~/Desktop/demo7/").














Time
Duration
Activity
Sections

10 min

Setup

4, 5, 6

The first 30 minutes (Setup through Section 3) tend to take longer than expected because of setwd() problems and package installation issues. Budget extra time here.
contMap rendering (Section 5) takes 5–15 seconds depending on the machine. Warn students it is not frozen.
If you are running behind, Section 7 (trunk marbling) can be shortened to just showing the plot without discussion. Do NOT cut Section 8 — the independent work is where the learning sticks.








Cause: setwd() path is wrong, or the data folder is not where they expect. Fix: Have them run getwd() to see where R thinks they are. Then check list.files("data/frederich/") to see if the files are visible. Most common issue: they are one directory level off.



Cause: The names on the trait vector don’t match the tree tip labels. Fix: Have them check head(names(pc)) and head(tree_matched$tip.label). The names must match exactly. Most common cause: they forgot names(pc) <- tr$species or used the unmatched data frame instead of tr.



Cause: Missing data (NA values) in the trait vector. Fix: Make sure they used na.rm = TRUE in the rowSums() call for pattern complexity. For individual traits, NAs should not be an issue since they are 0/1, but double-check with sum(is.na(my_trait)).



Cause: Confusion between significant signal and strong signal. Fix: K < 1 with p < 0.05 means there IS significant signal, but it is weaker than what you’d expect under pure Brownian motion. The tree predicts trait similarity, just not as strongly as a random-drift model would suggest. This is actually the most common result in nature — it means something like natural selection or convergence is partially overriding phylogenetic inertia.



Cause: The randomization test involves random permutations, so p-values will vary slightly between runs. Fix: This is expected. K itself should be identical (it is deterministic). Only the p-value changes. If they set a seed (set.seed(42)) before running, they will get identical results.



Cause: Lab machines sometimes have restricted write access. Fix: Try install.packages("phytools", lib = "~/R/library") and then library(phytools, lib.loc = "~/R/library"). Or install to a temp directory.






The golden rule (Section 3): This is the most important concept for their final projects. Emphasize that every comparative analysis starts with matching tree tips to data. Students who skip this step in Part 2 will get cryptic errors. Spend time on this even if it feels tedious.
K < 1 does not mean “no signal” (Section 6): This is the most common misinterpretation. Draw a quick diagram on the board: K = 0 (no signal, random with respect to tree) vs. K < 1 (some signal, weaker than BM) vs. K = 1 (matches BM exactly) vs. K > 1 (hyper-conserved). The p-value tells you whether signal is real; K tells you how strong it is.
contMap is an estimate, not data (Section 5): The colors along internal branches are inferred ancestral values, not observed data. We only have data at the tips. Students sometimes treat the internal colors as facts. Remind them that ancestral state estimation involves assumptions (especially the BM model).
Connection to Turing patterns (Section 7): The trunk marbling trait is a great bridge to Lecture 12. If marbling clusters in certain clades, it suggests the developmental toolkit for Turing patterns may be phylogenetically inherited, even if the specific pattern varies.
“Phylogenetically conserved” requires nuance (Deliverable): Push students to avoid black-and-white answers. The correct answer to “Is color pattern diversity conserved?” is “It depends on the trait and the scale.” Pattern complexity shows weak but significant signal. Some individual traits may show strong signal. Others may show none. That’s biology.








65 tips, root age ~65.3 Ma
Genera: Acanthurus, Ctenochaetus, Naso, Zebrasoma, Paracanthurus, Prionurus





62 species in common
3 in tree but not in data
21 in data but not in tree





Range: 4–19 pattern elements
Most complex: Acanthurus_bariene (19)
Simplest: Zebrasoma_rostratum (4)
Average by genus (approximate):

Acanthurus: n=26, mean ~10.2
Ctenochaetus: n=6, mean ~9.5
Naso: n=19, mean ~8.2
Zebrasoma: n=7, mean ~7.3
Paracanthurus: n=1 (single species)
Prionurus: n=3, mean ~6.7






The fan tree should show warm (red) colors concentrated in the Acanthurus clade, with cooler (blue) colors in Zebrasoma and Prionurus. Naso is mixed. There should be visible shifts along some branches.





Blomberg’s K = ~0.163, p = ~0.039 (significant)
Pagel’s lambda = ~0.294, p = ~0.019 (significant)
Both indicate weak but statistically significant phylogenetic signal. K well below 1 means the tree predicts complexity, but much less strongly than Brownian motion would predict.





17 of 62 species have trunk marbling
Concentrated in Acanthurus (11 of 26 species) and Ctenochaetus (3 of 6)
Absent in Zebrasoma (0/7), Prionurus (0/3), and Paracanthurus (0/1)
Visually clusters on the tree — not randomly distributed









Present in ~12 species
Largely overlaps with trunk marbling (same Turing-pattern mechanism)
Moderate K, likely significant
Clusters in Acanthurus and Ctenochaetus





Present in ~5–8 species
Scattered across clades — may NOT show strong signal
K likely non-significant (p > 0.05)
Good example of a trait that evolved independently multiple times





Present in ~10–15 species
The classic “eye bar” — common across multiple genera
May show moderate signal
Good for discussing anti-predator function (eye camouflage)





Present in ~10 species
The surgeonfish weapon — potentially an aposematic signal
Worth discussing: why would you advertise your weapon with color?
Signal strength variable





Rare — present in only ~3–5 species
Low sample size makes signal tests unreliable
Good teaching moment about statistical power with rare traits





Present in ~12–18 species
Common in Acanthurus
Likely shows significant signal
Classic “lateral stripe” — discuss camouflage function (breaking up body outline)






Remind students at the end of class:

Next week they will apply these same methods to their group’s fish clade
They will use color metrics extracted from images via pavo (not binary presence/absence like today)
The tree will come from the Brownstein et al. (2025) labrid phylogeny
Groups should make sure their pavo image analysis from Demo 5 is complete
The question shifts from “Is this trait conserved in surgeonfishes?” to “Is color diversity conserved in MY clade?”

title: Demo 7 — Mapping Color Patterns onto a Phylogeny
text:
Today you are going to do something that most biologists only encounter in graduate school: map traits onto a phylogenetic tree and ask whether evolutionary history predicts what organisms look like. Specifically, you will take a dataset of color pattern traits for surgeonfishes (family Acanthuridae) and a time-calibrated phylogeny, and combine them to ask: are closely related surgeonfishes more similar in their color patterns than you would expect by chance?
This question sits at the heart of comparative biology. If you know that two species are close relatives, can you predict that they share similar color patterns? Or do color patterns evolve so rapidly — driven by selection for camouflage, mate recognition, or species identity — that relatedness tells you almost nothing? The statistical tools we will use today, Blomberg’s K and Pagel’s lambda, give us a rigorous way to answer this. These metrics measure what comparative biologists call phylogenetic signal: the degree to which the phylogeny predicts trait similarity among species.
Don’t worry if this feels new. The code is all written for you — your job is to run it, look at the output, and think hard about what it means. By the end of class, you will be able to load a phylogeny, match it to trait data, visualize traits on a tree, and test for phylogenetic signal. Next week, in Part 2, you will apply these exact same methods to your group’s fish clade using image data you collected with pavo.

title: Demo 7 — Mapping Color Patterns onto a Phylogeny
text:
Setup
Before we begin, we need to load two R packages: ape (for reading and manipulating phylogenies) and phytools (for trait mapping and signal tests).






NoteFirst time? Install the packages first



If you have never used these packages before, run this line once in your R console:
install.packages(c("ape", "phytools"))
You only need to do this once per computer. After that, you just load them with library().



library(ape)       # reading and manipulating phylogenetic trees
library(phytools)  # trait mapping, contMap, phylogenetic signal tests

Now set your working directory to the folder that contains the data/ subfolder. This path will be different on your computer — adjust it to wherever you saved the demo materials.

# Change this path to match YOUR computer!
setwd("~/Desktop/demo7/")

Now create a folder to hold the figures you will generate today. Every plot in this worksheet will get saved to this folder as a PDF, so by the end of class you will have a tidy archive of your results.

# Create a results folder if it doesn't exist
if (!dir.exists("results")) dir.create("results")







TipWhy save PDFs?



When you view a plot in RStudio’s Plots pane, you are seeing a rasterized preview — it can look fuzzy or even drop thin lines if the pane is small. Saving to PDF writes vector graphics that stay sharp at any zoom level and that you can open, print, or include in a poster without losing quality.
You will see the same three-line pattern after every plot in this worksheet:
pdf("results/something.pdf", width = 10, height = 8)  # open a PDF device
plot(...)                                              # redraw the plot
dev.off()                                              # close the device
The PDF is only written when dev.off() runs. If you forget that line, R will hold the file open and the PDF will be empty or corrupted.

title: Demo 7 — Mapping Color Patterns onto a Phylogeny
section: Section 1: Loading and Plotting the Tree
text:
Section 1: Loading and Plotting the Tree
Our phylogeny comes from Frederich et al., who built a time-calibrated tree for surgeonfishes. “Time-calibrated” means the branch lengths are in millions of years — so you can read off approximately when lineages diverged.
Let’s load it and take a look.

tree <- read.nexus("data/frederich/acanthuridae-tree.tree")

# What did we just load?
tree


Phylogenetic tree with 65 tips and 64 internal nodes.

Tip labels:
  Acanthurus_achilles, Acanthurus_auranticavus, Acanthurus_bahianus, Acanthurus_bariene, Acanthurus_blochii, Acanthurus_chirurgus, ...

Rooted; includes branch length(s).



cat("Number of tips (species):", length(tree$tip.label), "\n")

Number of tips (species): 65 

cat("Root age:", round(max(branching.times(tree)), 1), "million years\n")

Root age: 65.3 million years

# What genera are represented?
genera <- unique(sub("_.*", "", tree$tip.label))
cat("Genera:", paste(genera, collapse = ", "), "\n")

Genera: Acanthurus, Ctenochaetus, Luvarus, Naso, Paracanthurus, Prionurus, Zanclus, Zebrasoma 


You should see 65 species and a root age of about 65 million years — right around the end-Cretaceous mass extinction. The major genera are Acanthurus (the largest), Naso (unicornfishes), Zebrasoma (tangs), Ctenochaetus (bristletooths), Prionurus, and Paracanthurus (that’s Dory).
Now let’s visualize it. First a rectangular tree, then a fan layout that fits better on screen.

plot(tree, cex = 0.45, no.margin = TRUE)








Save a PDF of the rectangular tree to your results folder:

pdf("results/acanthuridae-tree-rectangular.pdf", width = 10, height = 8)
plot(tree, cex = 0.45, no.margin = TRUE)
dev.off()


plotTree(tree, type = "fan", fsize = 0.5, lwd = 2.5)














WarningIf the fan tree looks empty or has only a few visible branches



The RStudio Plots pane can render thin lines at sub-pixel widths, which makes some branches disappear. If your fan tree looks mostly blank with only a couple of long branches visible (those go to the outgroups Luvarus and Zanclus), try one of these fixes:

Maximize the Plots pane before re-running the chunk (or drag its corner to make it bigger and roughly square).
Open the saved PDF (next chunk) — the PDF version always renders correctly because it uses vector graphics.
Bump lwd higher (e.g., lwd = 3 or lwd = 4) to force thicker lines.



Save a PDF of the fan tree:

pdf("results/acanthuridae-tree-fan.pdf", width = 10, height = 10)
plotTree(tree, type = "fan", fsize = 0.5, lwd = 2.5)
dev.off()

Take a moment. Open results/acanthuridae-tree-fan.pdf and zoom in. Can you spot where the major genera are? Acanthurus is the biggest cluster. Naso (unicornfishes) should be a distinct clade. Zebrasoma species cluster together. See if you can find Paracanthurus — the only species in its genus.

title: Demo 7 — Mapping Color Patterns onto a Phylogeny
text:
Section 2: Loading Color Pattern Data
Frederich et al. scored 52 binary color-pattern traits for 83 surgeonfish species. Each trait records whether a specific pattern element is present (1) or absent (0) on a particular body region.

traits <- read.csv("data/frederich/acanthuridae-color-patterns.csv",
                    stringsAsFactors = FALSE, check.names = FALSE)

cat("Species in dataset:", nrow(traits), "\n")

Species in dataset: 83 

cat("Total columns:", ncol(traits), "\n")

Total columns: 59 








TipWhat do those column names mean?



The trait columns use a prefix coding system based on body region:



Prefix
Body region
Examples




H_
Head
H_marbling, H_1_v_strip, H_eyespot


Tr_
Trunk (body)
Tr_1_h_strip, Tr_marbling, Tr_eyespot


Ta_
Tail
Ta_saddle, Ta_colored_scalpel, Ta_eyespot



So Tr_marbling means “irregular worm-like markings on the trunk” and Ta_colored_scalpel means “the caudal spine (the surgeonfish’s famous tail blade) is distinctly colored.”
The first 7 columns are metadata: a row number, the species name, and five biogeographic region codes (CIP, WI, CP, A, TEP). The pattern traits start at column 8.


Let’s peek at the column names to confirm:

title: Demo 7 — Mapping Color Patterns onto a Phylogeny
text:
Section 3: Matching Tree to Data
Here is the golden rule of comparative phylogenetics:






ImportantThe Golden Rule



The species in your tree and the species in your data must match exactly. Every species in your analysis needs to appear in both the tree and the trait table. Species that are in one but not the other get dropped.
This sounds obvious, but it is the single most common source of errors in comparative analyses. Names must match exactly — Acanthurus_achilles is not the same as Acanthurus achilles or A. achilles.


Let’s check the overlap:

overlap <- intersect(tree$tip.label, traits$species)
cat("Species in BOTH tree and data:", length(overlap), "\n")

Species in BOTH tree and data: 62 

cat("In tree but NOT in data:      ", length(setdiff(tree$tip.label, traits$species)), "\n")

In tree but NOT in data:       3 

cat("In data but NOT in tree:      ", length(setdiff(traits$species, tree$tip.label)), "\n")

In data but NOT in tree:       21 


We have 62 species in common. That means 3 species are in the tree but lack color data, and 21 species have color data but are not in the tree. This is completely normal — real datasets never have perfect overlap.
Now we prune the tree and filter the data so they match:

# Prune the tree to keep only species with data
tree_matched <- drop.tip(tree, setdiff(tree$tip.label, overlap))
cat("Matched tree:", length(tree_matched$tip.label), "tips\n")

Matched tree: 62 tips

# Filter the trait table to keep only species in the tree
tr <- traits[traits$species %in% overlap, ]
cat("Matched data:", nrow(tr), "rows\n")

Matched data: 62 rows


From here on, tree_matched and tr are our working objects. They have exactly the same 62 species.

title: Demo 7 — Mapping Color Patterns onto a Phylogeny
text:
Section 4: Pattern Complexity
Before we look at individual traits, let’s create a summary variable: how many total color pattern elements does each species have? This is a rough measure of how “visually complex” a surgeonfish is. A species with 18 pattern elements has stripes, spots, marbling, and eyespots all over its body. A species with 4 elements is relatively plain.

# Sum all binary pattern traits (columns 8 onward)
pattern_cols <- names(tr)[8:ncol(tr)]
tr$pattern_complexity <- rowSums(tr[, pattern_cols], na.rm = TRUE)

# Who's most complex? Who's simplest?
cat("Most complex:", tr$species[which.max(tr$pattern_complexity)],
    "—", max(tr$pattern_complexity), "elements\n")

Most complex: Acanthurus_bariene — 19 elements

cat("Simplest:    ", tr$species[which.min(tr$pattern_complexity)],
    "—", min(tr$pattern_complexity), "elements\n")

Simplest:     Zebrasoma_rostratum — 4 elements



hist(tr$pattern_complexity, breaks = 12, col = "#6fa8dc",
     main = "Color pattern complexity across surgeonfishes",
     xlab = "Number of pattern elements", ylab = "Number of species")








Save a PDF copy of the histogram:

pdf("results/pattern-complexity-histogram.pdf", width = 8, height = 5)
hist(tr$pattern_complexity, breaks = 12, col = "#6fa8dc",
     main = "Color pattern complexity across surgeonfishes",
     xlab = "Number of pattern elements", ylab = "Number of species")
dev.off()

Most species fall in the 7–12 range, but there is a long right tail of highly ornamented species. Let’s compare genera:

cat("\n=== Average complexity by genus ===\n")


=== Average complexity by genus ===

genus <- sub("_.*", "", tr$species)
for (g in unique(genus)) {
  vals <- tr$pattern_complexity[genus == g]
  cat(sprintf("  %-15s  n = %2d   mean = %.1f\n", g, length(vals), mean(vals)))
}

  Acanthurus       n = 26   mean = 11.2
  Ctenochaetus     n =  6   mean = 11.0
  Prionurus        n =  6   mean = 8.7
  Paracanthurus    n =  1   mean = 8.0
  Zebrasoma        n =  4   mean = 7.2
  Naso             n = 19   mean = 8.7








NoteDiscussion question



Which genus has the highest average pattern complexity? Which has the lowest? Why might some genera be more ornamented than others? Think about what you know about surgeonfish ecology — reef habitat, social behavior, diet, predator interactions.

title: Demo 7 — Mapping Color Patterns onto a Phylogeny
section: Section 5: Mapping Complexity onto the Tree (contMap)
text:
Section 5: Mapping Complexity onto the Tree (contMap)
Now comes the fun part. The contMap function in phytools “paints” a continuous trait along the branches of the phylogeny. It estimates ancestral values at internal nodes using maximum likelihood and then interpolates along each branch, producing a smooth color gradient from tips to root. This lets you see where on the tree the trait changed.

# Create a named vector — phytools requires names to match tip labels
pc <- tr$pattern_complexity
names(pc) <- tr$species

# Generate the contMap object
cm <- contMap(tree_matched, pc, plot = FALSE)

# Plot it as a fan tree
plot(cm, type = "fan", fsize = 0.5, lwd = 3,
     legend = 0.7 * max(nodeHeights(tree_matched)))
title("Color pattern complexity mapped on Acanthuridae phylogeny")








Save a PDF of the contMap. This is the figure you will submit as part of your deliverable:

pdf("results/pattern-complexity-contmap.pdf", width = 10, height = 10)
plot(cm, type = "fan", fsize = 0.5, lwd = 3,
     legend = 0.7 * max(nodeHeights(tree_matched)))
title("Color pattern complexity mapped on Acanthuridae phylogeny")
dev.off()







TipWhat to look for




Cool colors (blue/purple) = simple patterns (few elements)
Warm colors (red/yellow) = complex patterns (many elements)
Do closely related species tend to be the same color? That would suggest phylogenetic signal.
Can you spot any shifts — branches where the color changes abruptly from cool to warm or vice versa?
Do any genera stand out as consistently complex or consistently simple?









NoteDiscussion question



Look at your contMap. Can you identify one or two branches where pattern complexity appears to shift dramatically? What might have driven those shifts? (Think about habitat changes, shifts in social behavior, or changes in predation regime.)

title: Demo 7 — Mapping Color Patterns onto a Phylogeny
text:
Section 6: Testing Phylogenetic Signal
Your contMap gives you a visual impression, but we need statistics to back it up. We will use two complementary tests.

Blomberg’s K
Blomberg’s K compares the observed trait variance among species to the variance expected under Brownian motion (a model where traits drift randomly along the tree).







K value
Interpretation




K = 1
Trait matches Brownian motion exactly


K > 1
Relatives are more similar than BM predicts (strong phylogenetic conservatism)


K < 1
Relatives are less similar than BM predicts (trait is more labile, possibly driven by selection or convergence)


K near 0
No relationship between the tree and the trait



The p-value comes from a randomization test: the trait values are shuffled across the tips 1,000 times, and the observed K is compared to that null distribution. If p < 0.05, the signal is statistically significant.

K_result <- phylosig(tree_matched, pc, method = "K", test = TRUE)
K_result


Phylogenetic signal K : 0.162505 
P-value (based on 1000 randomizations) : 0.033 

cat("\n--- YOUR INTERPRETATION ---\n")


--- YOUR INTERPRETATION ---

cat("Blomberg's K =", round(K_result$K, 3), "\n")

Blomberg's K = 0.163 

cat("p-value      =", round(K_result$P, 4), "\n")

p-value      = 0.033 

if (K_result$P < 0.05) {
  cat("Result: SIGNIFICANT phylogenetic signal.\n")
  cat("Closely related surgeonfishes DO have more similar color complexity.\n")
} else {
  cat("Result: No significant signal.\n")
  cat("Color complexity is NOT strongly predicted by relatedness.\n")
}

Result: SIGNIFICANT phylogenetic signal.
Closely related surgeonfishes DO have more similar color complexity.








NoteFill in this interpretation template



“My Blomberg’s K value was _____, which means that closely related species are [more / less] similar in pattern complexity than expected under Brownian motion. This signal [is / is not] statistically significant (p = _____). This suggests that color pattern complexity in surgeonfishes [is / is not] phylogenetically conserved.”




Pagel’s lambda
Pagel’s lambda asks the same fundamental question but approaches it differently. Lambda transforms the tree’s internal branch lengths to find the degree of phylogenetic signal that best fits the data.







Lambda value
Interpretation




Lambda = 1
The tree perfectly predicts trait similarity (full signal)


Lambda = 0
The tree is irrelevant — equivalent to a star phylogeny where all species are equally related


0 < Lambda < 1
Partial signal — some phylogenetic structure, but not as much as BM predicts




lambda_result <- phylosig(tree_matched, pc, method = "lambda", test = TRUE)
lambda_result


Phylogenetic signal lambda : 0.29401 
logL(lambda) : -155.306 
LR(lambda=0) : 5.54131 
P-value (based on LR test) : 0.0185727 

cat("\nPagel's lambda =", round(lambda_result$lambda, 3), "\n")


Pagel's lambda = 0.294 

cat("p-value        =", round(lambda_result$P, 4), "\n")

p-value        = 0.0186 


Do K and lambda agree? They often give slightly different values because they are sensitive to different aspects of the data. K is more sensitive to tip-level similarity, while lambda is more sensitive to deep phylogenetic structure. If both are significant, you can be confident that there is real phylogenetic signal.

title: Demo 7 — Mapping Color Patterns onto a Phylogeny
section: Section 7: Mapping a Discrete Trait — Trunk Marbling
text:
Section 7: Mapping a Discrete Trait — Trunk Marbling
So far we have worked with a continuous summary variable (pattern complexity). Now let’s look at a single, specific color pattern element: trunk marbling. This is the irregular, worm-like patterning on the body that connects directly to the Turing patterns we discussed in Lecture 12. Turing patterns arise from reaction-diffusion dynamics between activator and inhibitor molecules during development — the same process that produces leopard spots, zebra stripes, and the labyrinthine patterns on some surgeonfishes.

marbling <- tr$Tr_marbling
names(marbling) <- tr$species

cat("Trunk marbling present:", sum(marbling), "of", length(marbling), "species\n")

Trunk marbling present: 17 of 62 species

# Color the tree tips by marbling presence
cols <- c("0" = "#6fa8dc", "1" = "#e07a5f")
plot(tree_matched, type = "fan", fsize = 0.45, lwd = 2.5,
     tip.color = cols[as.character(marbling[tree_matched$tip.label])])
legend("topleft",
       legend = c("No trunk marbling", "Trunk marbling present"),
       fill = c("#6fa8dc", "#e07a5f"), cex = 0.9, bty = "n")
title("Trunk marbling in Acanthuridae")








Save the trunk-marbling tree as a PDF:

pdf("results/trunk-marbling-fan.pdf", width = 10, height = 10)
plot(tree_matched, type = "fan", fsize = 0.45, lwd = 2.5,
     tip.color = cols[as.character(marbling[tree_matched$tip.label])])

Warning in plot.window(...): "fsize" is not a graphical parameter


Warning in plot.xy(xy, type, ...): "fsize" is not a graphical parameter


Warning in title(...): "fsize" is not a graphical parameter

legend("topleft",
       legend = c("No trunk marbling", "Trunk marbling present"),
       fill = c("#6fa8dc", "#e07a5f"), cex = 0.9, bty = "n")
title("Trunk marbling in Acanthuridae")
dev.off()







NoteDiscussion questions




Does trunk marbling cluster on the tree, or is it scattered randomly across unrelated species?
Which genera seem to have the most trunk marbling? Which have none at all?
How many times do you think trunk marbling evolved independently?
Could this pattern be a Turing-generated trait? What would that imply about the developmental genetics underlying it?

text:

title: Demo 7 — Mapping Color Patterns onto a Phylogeny
text:
Part 2 — Your Group’s Three Species
In Part 1 you mapped color-pattern traits onto someone else’s clade (Acanthuridae). In Part 2 you’ll do the same kind of plot for your own group’s fish family — but with actual images of fish at the tips of the tree. By the end of this section you will:

log into the FishView server using your group’s code,
pick three species from three different genera in your family,
star (★) one exemplar image per species,
export a Part 2 bundle (3 images + a phylogeny), and
run an R script that renders the pruned tree with your three exemplar fish pinned at the tips.

The goal is simple. Once you see your three species sitting at the tips of an actual phylogeny, side-by-side with their photographs, you should be able to look at the image and ask: do my species’ color patterns look more similar within a genus than across genera?

Step 1 — Log into FishView
The FishView server runs on the lab Mac mini and is only reachable from the UCLA campus network or UCLA Bruin Secure VPN.

Open http://164.67.78.200:5052/ in your browser.
Enter your group code (see the table below) and your first name.
You’ll land on your group’s species dashboard.




Group
Family
Code




1
Balistoidea (triggerfishes)
trigger-2026


2
Pomacanthidae (angelfishes)
angel-2026


3
Scarinae (parrotfishes)
parrot-2026


4
Cirrhilabrini (fairy wrasses)
fairy-2026


5
Acanthuridae (surgeonfishes)
tang-2026


6
Hypsigenyinae (hogfishes)
hog-2026


7
Halichoerine (Coris-allies wrasses)
coris-2026









WarningIf the page won’t load



You’re probably off-campus without VPN. Connect to UCLA Bruin Secure VPN first, then refresh. The server has no public DNS name — there is no way to reach it from outside UCLA’s network.




Step 2 — Pick three species from three different genera
Why three different genera? Because the point of putting species on a tree is to show phylogenetic spread. If you pick three species of Acanthurus, you’ll get a tree that’s all one tiny twig — that’s not a comparative analysis, that’s three siblings. Picking three different genera guarantees your tree has real branching depth, and lets you see whether color patterns diverge as you move from genus to genus.






ImportantPick from the highlighted (🌳) species only



Your dashboard now shows a green 🌳 chip next to species names that appear in your group’s phylogeny. You must pick three highlighted species. If you star an exemplar for a species that’s NOT in the tree, the Part 2 plot in Step 5 will silently skip it.
There’s also a “🌳 Only species in the tree” filter toggle in the controls row — flip it on to hide everything else and only see your candidates.




Step 3 — Star (★) one exemplar image per species
For each of your three picked species:

Click the species name to open the review page.
Scroll through the available images. Exclude bad ones (back-facing fish, juveniles, weird angles) using the Exclude button.
Pick the one image that best represents the typical adult color pattern for that species, and click the ★ Exemplar radio button. That’s the image Part 2 will use.
Optionally set the Gestalt-K value (number of perceptually distinct colors, 2–10).
Hit “Save & Next species” or use the dropdown.

This is the same workflow you used last week — you’re just doing it for three species instead of all of them.


Step 4 — Export the Part 2 bundle
Once all three species have a ★ exemplar marked, click the green “Export Part 2 Bundle” button at the top of the dashboard. The server packages everything into a ZIP file called <family>-part2-bundle.zip:
<family>-part2-bundle.zip
├── images/
│   ├── Genus_species_1.png
│   ├── Genus_species_2.png
│   └── Genus_species_3.png
├── <family>-tree.tre           ← Newick phylogeny for your family
├── picked_species.txt          ← the 3 species names
├── picked_tip_labels.txt       ← exact tree-tip labels (so R can prune)
└── README.txt
Unzip it into your demo folder under data/my-fish/ (the folder structure must look like the tree above, with the ZIP’s contents inside data/my-fish/).
# In RStudio: this assumes you've moved the ZIP into your demo folder
unzip("<family>-part2-bundle.zip", exdir = "data/my-fish")
list.files("data/my-fish", recursive = TRUE)


Step 5 — Render the tree with your exemplar images at the tips
The R code below loads your three images and the pruned family tree, then plots the tree with each fish photo sitting at its corresponding tip. The tree comes from the same kind of phylogenetic analysis you ran in Part 1 — we just stripped it down to your three picked species.

# Load the helper that does the heavy lifting
source("scripts/part2-tree-with-images.R")

# Adjust `family` to your group's family (lowercase, no spaces) and the
# bundle directory to wherever you unzipped:
plot_part2(
  family     = "acanthuridae",         # e.g. cirrhilabrini, scarinae, halichoerine, hypsigenyinae
  bundle_dir = "data/my-fish",
  out_pdf    = "results/part2-tree-with-images.pdf"
)

What plot_part2() does, step by step:

Reads the Newick tree from data/my-fish/<family>-tree.tre.
Prunes it to just your three picked tip labels using ape::keep.tip() — the same function drop.tip() from Part 1, in reverse.
Plots the pruned tree with ape::plot.phylo().
Pulls the tip x/y coordinates from .PlotPhyloEnv (a hidden environment that plot.phylo populates with the last plot’s layout).
Places each fish image at its tip using rasterImage() — this is base-R’s way to draw a raster (bitmap) at given coordinates.
Labels each image with the italicized species name underneath.
Saves the result as a vector PDF.







TipWhat to look for



Open results/part2-tree-with-images.pdf and put it next to your group’s three exemplar photos. Then ask:

Are the two species in the same genus (if you happened to pick two from the same genus) more similar in pattern than the outgroup species?
Do closely related species (joined by a deeper branch) look like obvious sister-species in terms of color, or do they look completely different?
Are any of the patterns from Part 1 (stripes, bars, eyespots, marbling) shared across all three of your tips? Across just two? Across none?









NoteDiscussion question



In a single sentence: do your three species’ color patterns appear conserved along the phylogeny, or do they look like independent paint jobs with no obvious phylogenetic structure? Write the answer down — you’ll need it for the Deliverable.




What if my group doesn’t have a tree?
Two of the seven family-level groups (Pomacanthidae and Balistoidea) have inventories on FishView but no phylogeny in this demo release. If that’s your group:

Still do Steps 1–3 (log in, pick 3 species from 3 genera, star exemplars).
Skip Step 4 (no Export-Bundle button will appear).
For Step 5, borrow the Acanthuridae bundle in data/frederich/ and three demo Acanthuridae images so you can produce the figure for the deliverable. Note in your write-up that the figure uses surrogate data because your family’s tree isn’t yet available.







WarningIf plot_part2() says “fewer than 2 of your picked tips were found”



This usually means you picked a species that isn’t in the highlighted list. Go back to the dashboard, turn on the “🌳 Only species in the tree” filter, and re-pick three highlighted species. Then re-export the bundle and re-run.

title: Demo 7 — Mapping Color Patterns onto a Phylogeny
text:
Deliverable
Submit the following on BruinLearn by Friday May 22 at 11:59 PM:

results/pattern-complexity-contmap.pdf from Section 5 (the colored fan tree).
Your K and lambda values with a one-sentence interpretation of each (use the template from Section 6).
Your “Your Turn” results: which trait you chose, K value, p-value, and your results/my-trait-fan.pdf (renamed to match your trait).
One paragraph (4–6 sentences) answering the question: “Is color pattern diversity phylogenetically conserved in surgeonfishes?” Your answer should be nuanced — some traits may show signal while others do not. Why might that be?
Part 2: results/part2-tree-with-images.pdf — the pruned tree with your three group exemplars at the tips.
Part 2 interpretation (2 sentences): Do your three species’ color patterns appear conserved within your family’s phylogeny, or do they look randomly distributed? Use what you see in your figure as evidence.







TipWhere are your files?



Everything you generated today lives in your results/ folder:
results/
├── acanthuridae-tree-rectangular.pdf
├── acanthuridae-tree-fan.pdf
├── pattern-complexity-histogram.pdf
├── pattern-complexity-contmap.pdf
├── trunk-marbling-fan.pdf
├── my-trait-fan.pdf                 ← yours from Section 8
└── part2-tree-with-images.pdf       ← Part 2, your group's three exemplars
If a file is missing, scroll back to the corresponding chunk and re-run it — the dev.off() line is what actually writes the PDF to disk. If a PDF is empty or won’t open, you likely forgot dev.off() and need to re-run the chunk from scratch.

title: Demo 7 — Mapping Color Patterns onto a Phylogeny
text:
In this demo you’ll learn to map color pattern traits onto a phylogenetic tree and test whether closely related surgeonfish species have more similar color patterns than expected by chance.
Organism: Acanthuridae (surgeonfishes, tangs, unicornfishes) — 62 species
Data: 52 binary color pattern traits (stripes, spots, eyespots, marbling, etc.) coded across head, trunk, and tail body regions, from Frederich et al.
Methods: contMap, Blomberg’s K, Pagel’s λ, discrete trait mapping

title: Demo 7 — TA Guide
text:
EEB 187: Ecology and Evolution of Color Week 8, Wednesday May 20, 2026 Duration: 75 minutes




R packages installed on all lab machines: ape, phytools. Test with library(ape); library(phytools) — no errors.
Data files in place on all machines or on the shared drive. The folder structure should be:
demo7/
  data/
    frederich/
      acanthuridae-tree.tree    (NEXUS format, 65 tips)
      acanthuridae-color-patterns.csv   (83 species, 59 columns)
  demo7-worksheet.qmd
Test-render the QMD on at least one machine: quarto render demo7-worksheet.qmd --to html. Confirm that all chunks execute, especially the contMap and phylosig calls (these take a few seconds each).
Print 5–6 paper copies of the QMD (or have the HTML open on the podium) for students who prefer to follow along on paper.
Projector works with RStudio visible. You will want to live-code Sections 1–3 while students follow along.
setwd() paths: Students will need to change the setwd() path. Have the correct path for the lab machines written on the board or in a pinned Slack message before class starts. Something like: setwd("C:/Users/student/Desktop/demo7/") or setwd("~/Desktop/demo7/").














Time
Duration
Activity
Sections




0:00–0:10
10 min
Instructor intro: What is a phylogeny? What is phylogenetic signal? Why do we care? Use slides if available. Key message: “We’re asking whether evolutionary history predicts color patterns.”
—


0:10–0:15
5 min
Students open RStudio, load packages, set working directory. Walk around and troubleshoot setwd() issues.
Setup


0:15–0:30
15 min
Guided walkthrough (together): Load tree, plot it, load trait data, match tree to data. Do this at the front while students follow along. Pause after the matching step to emphasize the golden rule.
1, 2, 3


0:30–0:45
15 min
Guided walkthrough (together): Create complexity score, histogram, genus comparison. Then contMap and phylogenetic signal tests. Let students run each chunk, then discuss results as a class.
4, 5, 6


0:45–0:55
10 min
Discrete trait mapping: Trunk marbling. Brief discussion about Turing patterns connecting back to Lecture 12.
7


0:55–1:05
10 min
Independent work: Students choose a trait and repeat the analysis. TAs circulate and help.
8


1:05–1:15
10 min
Wrap-up discussion: Call on 2–3 students to share their trait results. Remind about the deliverable. Preview Part 2 (next week: their own clade with pavo data).
—






The first 30 minutes (Setup through Section 3) tend to take longer than expected because of setwd() problems and package installation issues. Budget extra time here.
contMap rendering (Section 5) takes 5–15 seconds depending on the machine. Warn students it is not frozen.
If you are running behind, Section 7 (trunk marbling) can be shortened to just showing the plot without discussion. Do NOT cut Section 8 — the independent work is where the learning sticks.








Cause: setwd() path is wrong, or the data folder is not where they expect. Fix: Have them run getwd() to see where R thinks they are. Then check list.files("data/frederich/") to see if the files are visible. Most common issue: they are one directory level off.



Cause: The names on the trait vector don’t match the tree tip labels. Fix: Have them check head(names(pc)) and head(tree_matched$tip.label). The names must match exactly. Most common cause: they forgot names(pc) <- tr$species or used the unmatched data frame instead of tr.



Cause: Missing data (NA values) in the trait vector. Fix: Make sure they used na.rm = TRUE in the rowSums() call for pattern complexity. For individual traits, NAs should not be an issue since they are 0/1, but double-check with sum(is.na(my_trait)).



Cause: Confusion between significant signal and strong signal. Fix: K < 1 with p < 0.05 means there IS significant signal, but it is weaker than what you’d expect under pure Brownian motion. The tree predicts trait similarity, just not as strongly as a random-drift model would suggest. This is actually the most common result in nature — it means something like natural selection or convergence is partially overriding phylogenetic inertia.



Cause: The randomization test involves random permutations, so p-values will vary slightly between runs. Fix: This is expected. K itself should be identical (it is deterministic). Only the p-value changes. If they set a seed (set.seed(42)) before running, they will get identical results.



Cause: Lab machines sometimes have restricted write access. Fix: Try install.packages("phytools", lib = "~/R/library") and then library(phytools, lib.loc = "~/R/library"). Or install to a temp directory.






The golden rule (Section 3): This is the most important concept for their final projects. Emphasize that every comparative analysis starts with matching tree tips to data. Students who skip this step in Part 2 will get cryptic errors. Spend time on this even if it feels tedious.
K < 1 does not mean “no signal” (Section 6): This is the most common misinterpretation. Draw a quick diagram on the board: K = 0 (no signal, random with respect to tree) vs. K < 1 (some signal, weaker than BM) vs. K = 1 (matches BM exactly) vs. K > 1 (hyper-conserved). The p-value tells you whether signal is real; K tells you how strong it is.
contMap is an estimate, not data (Section 5): The colors along internal branches are inferred ancestral values, not observed data. We only have data at the tips. Students sometimes treat the internal colors as facts. Remind them that ancestral state estimation involves assumptions (especially the BM model).
Connection to Turing patterns (Section 7): The trunk marbling trait is a great bridge to Lecture 12. If marbling clusters in certain clades, it suggests the developmental toolkit for Turing patterns may be phylogenetically inherited, even if the specific pattern varies.
“Phylogenetically conserved” requires nuance (Deliverable): Push students to avoid black-and-white answers. The correct answer to “Is color pattern diversity conserved?” is “It depends on the trait and the scale.” Pattern complexity shows weak but significant signal. Some individual traits may show strong signal. Others may show none. That’s biology.








65 tips, root age ~65.3 Ma
Genera: Acanthurus, Ctenochaetus, Naso, Zebrasoma, Paracanthurus, Prionurus





62 species in common
3 in tree but not in data
21 in data but not in tree





Range: 4–19 pattern elements
Most complex: Acanthurus_bariene (19)
Simplest: Zebrasoma_rostratum (4)
Average by genus (approximate):

Acanthurus: n=26, mean ~10.2
Ctenochaetus: n=6, mean ~9.5
Naso: n=19, mean ~8.2
Zebrasoma: n=7, mean ~7.3
Paracanthurus: n=1 (single species)
Prionurus: n=3, mean ~6.7






The fan tree should show warm (red) colors concentrated in the Acanthurus clade, with cooler (blue) colors in Zebrasoma and Prionurus. Naso is mixed. There should be visible shifts along some branches.





Blomberg’s K = ~0.163, p = ~0.039 (significant)
Pagel’s lambda = ~0.294, p = ~0.019 (significant)
Both indicate weak but statistically significant phylogenetic signal. K well below 1 means the tree predicts complexity, but much less strongly than Brownian motion would predict.





17 of 62 species have trunk marbling
Concentrated in Acanthurus (11 of 26 species) and Ctenochaetus (3 of 6)
Absent in Zebrasoma (0/7), Prionurus (0/3), and Paracanthurus (0/1)
Visually clusters on the tree — not randomly distributed









Present in ~12 species
Largely overlaps with trunk marbling (same Turing-pattern mechanism)
Moderate K, likely significant
Clusters in Acanthurus and Ctenochaetus





Present in ~5–8 species
Scattered across clades — may NOT show strong signal
K likely non-significant (p > 0.05)
Good example of a trait that evolved independently multiple times





Present in ~10–15 species
The classic “eye bar” — common across multiple genera
May show moderate signal
Good for discussing anti-predator function (eye camouflage)





Present in ~10 species
The surgeonfish weapon — potentially an aposematic signal
Worth discussing: why would you advertise your weapon with color?
Signal strength variable





Rare — present in only ~3–5 species
Low sample size makes signal tests unreliable
Good teaching moment about statistical power with rare traits





Present in ~12–18 species
Common in Acanthurus
Likely shows significant signal
Classic “lateral stripe” — discuss camouflage function (breaking up body outline)






Remind students at the end of class:

Next week they will apply these same methods to their group’s fish clade
They will use color metrics extracted from images via pavo (not binary presence/absence like today)
The tree will come from the Brownstein et al. (2025) labrid phylogeny
Groups should make sure their pavo image analysis from Demo 5 is complete
The question shifts from “Is this trait conserved in surgeonfishes?” to “Is color diversity conserved in MY clade?”

title: Brownstein Labrid Tree — Handoff for FishView Image Agent
text:
Purpose: The FishView image server agent needs to populate family image directories for the 4 labrid project groups. Each group needs lateral-view photographs matched to species in the Brownstein et al. (2025) phylogeny. This handoff provides the tree, the species lists, and the matching rules.



Citation: Brownstein, C. D., et al. (2025). Phylogenomics establishes an Early Miocene reconstruction of reef vertebrate diversity. Science Advances, 11, eadu6149. https://doi.org/10.1126/sciadv.adu6149
Dryad: https://doi.org/10.5061/dryad.f7m0cfz5x
File: labs/demo-labrid-pcm-week8/data/labrid-time-calibrated-tree.tre (NEXUS format)
Stats: 415 tips, time-calibrated (GTR + node-dating, 13 fossil calibrations), root age 70.2 Ma. Covers all major labrid subfamilies + outgroups.




These 4 of the 7 class project groups use subclades of the Brownstein tree. The other 3 groups (Balistoidea, Pomacanthidae, Acanthuridae) use family-specific trees from other sources.









Field
Value




Students
Aghasi, Flo, Paul


Tips in tree
79


MRCA age
23.7 Ma


Genera
Scarus (37), Chlorurus (12), Sparisoma (13), Bolbometopon (1), Calotomus (3), Cetoscarus (2), Cryptotomus (1), Hipposcarus (1), Leptoscarus (1), Nicholsina (1)


Pruned tree file
data/clades/scarinae-tree.tre


Tip label CSV
data/clades/scarinae-tip-labels.csv


Color story
Extreme sexual dichromatism (initial phase → terminal phase). Terminal males vivid blue-green. Pharyngeal jaw innovation.


FishView priority
Need IP (initial phase) and TP (terminal phase) images per species













Field
Value




Students
Nancy, Alyssa, Leewon


Tips in tree
58


MRCA age
10.8 Ma


Genera
Cirrhilabrus (51), Paracheilinus (5), Conniella (1), Clepticus (1)


Pruned tree file
data/clades/cirrhilabrinae-tree.tre


Tip label CSV
data/clades/cirrhilabrini-tip-labels.csv


Color story
Most chromatically diverse labrid clade. Extreme sexual dichromatism. Males flash iridescent displays during courtship. Recent rapid radiation (~10.8 Ma).


FishView priority
Male display coloration is the primary signal of interest. Many species described in last 20 years — images may be scarce for rare species.


Note
The tree has duplicate tips for some species with different voucher codes (e.g., Cirrhilabrus_cyanopleuraQF180 and Cirrhilabrus_cyanopleuraF614KB16b). Image matching should use genus + species only.













Field
Value




Students
Edward, Hooman


Tips in tree
38


MRCA age
39.9 Ma


Genera
Bodianus (18), Choerodon (15), Semicossyphus (2), Achoerodus (1), Lachnolaimus (1)


Pruned tree file
data/clades/hypsigenyinae-tree.tre (needs to be generated — run prune-clades.R with Hypsigenyinae genera)


Tip label CSV
data/clades/hypsigenyinae-tip-labels.csv


Color story
Large-bodied wrasses with bold color blocks. Strong ontogenetic and sex-based color change.


FishView priority
Smaller clade — should be straightforward. ~20 unique species with images needed.













Field
Value




Students
Sophia, Jordyn


Tips in tree
140


MRCA age
27.3 Ma


Genera
Halichoeres (48), Coris (17), Thalassoma (13), Anampses (11), Stethojulis (6), Macropharyngodon (9), Hologymnosus (3), Hemigymnus (2), Pseudojuloides (3), Pseudocoris (2), Leptojulis (1), Ophthalmolepis (1), Suezichthys (3)


Pruned tree file
data/clades/halichoerine-tree.tre (needs to be generated)


Tip label CSV
data/clades/halichoerine-tip-labels.csv


Color story
Largest monophyletic labrid clade. Extreme ontogenetic and sexual color change. If the fish is green, predict mixed mechanism (iridophore + xanthophore).


FishView priority
LARGEST image need (~140 species). Prioritize: 10 Halichoeres, 5 Thalassoma, 5 Coris, 3 Anampses, 2 Macropharyngodon for initial set.








Tree tip labels have the format: Genus_speciesVOUCHER
Examples:
Scarus_ghobbanF204
Cirrhilabrus_scottorumF628KB9
Halichoeres_melanotisF72
Bodianus_rufus_F112
To match images to tree tips: 1. Extract genus + species: sub("([A-Z][a-z]+_[a-z]+).*", "\\1", tip_label) 2. This gives Scarus_ghobban, Cirrhilabrus_scottorum, etc. 3. Match to image filename: genus-species.jpg (lowercase, hyphenated) on FishView
Beware: - Some species have multiple tips (different specimens/vouchers). The image agent should provide ONE image per species — the R script will handle deduplication. - Some tip labels have underscores in unexpected places (e.g., Bodianus_rufus_F112 — the species is rufus, not rufus_F112). The regex above handles this.




For each of the 4 labrid family directories on FishView:
~/fishview-data/families/<family>/
├── images_fishwise/     ← FishWise lateral views (preferred)
├── images_fishbase/     ← FishBase photos
├── images_inaturalist/  ← iNaturalist CC photos
└── segmented/           ← alpha-masked PNGs (post-processing)
Minimum viable set for Demo 7 Part 2: 10 species per group, spanning genera. The tip label CSVs list every species in the tree — the agent should prioritize species that are: 1. In the tree (required) 2. Visually distinctive (good for color analysis) 3. Spread across genera (not all one genus)

title: Brownstein Labrid Tree — Handoff for FishView Image Agent
text:
These 4 of the 7 class project groups use subclades of the Brownstein tree. The other 3 groups (Balistoidea, Pomacanthidae, Acanthuridae) use family-specific trees from other sources.

Pruned tree file
data/clades/scarinae-tree.tre

data/clades/scarinae-tip-labels.csv


Color story
Extreme sexual dichromatism (initial phase → terminal phase). Terminal males vivid blue-green. Pharyngeal jaw innovation.


FishView priority
Need IP (initial phase) and TP (terminal phase) images per species

Pruned tree file
data/clades/cirrhilabrinae-tree.tre


Tip label CSV
data/clades/cirrhilabrini-tip-labels.csv


Color story
Most chromatically diverse labrid clade. Extreme sexual dichromatism. Males flash iridescent displays during courtship. Recent rapid radiation (~10.8 Ma).


FishView priority
Male display coloration is the primary signal of interest. Many species described in last 20 years — images may be scarce for rare species.


Note
The tree has duplicate tips for some species with different voucher codes (e.g., Cirrhilabrus_cyanopleuraQF180 and Cirrhilabrus_cyanopleuraF614KB16b). Image matching should use genus + species only.

Color story
Largest monophyletic labrid clade. Extreme ontogenetic and sexual color change. If the fish is green, predict mixed mechanism (iridophore + xanthophore).


FishView priority
LARGEST image need (~140 species). Prioritize: 10 Halichoeres, 5 Thalassoma, 5 Coris, 3 Anampses, 2 Macropharyngodon for initial set.

title: Brownstein Labrid Tree — Handoff for FishView Image Agent
text:
Tree tip labels have the format: Genus_speciesVOUCHER
Examples:
Scarus_ghobbanF204
Cirrhilabrus_scottorumF628KB9
Halichoeres_melanotisF72
Bodianus_rufus_F112
To match images to tree tips: 1. Extract genus + species: sub("([A-Z][a-z]+_[a-z]+).*", "\\1", tip_label) 2. This gives Scarus_ghobban, Cirrhilabrus_scottorum, etc. 3. Match to image filename: genus-species.jpg (lowercase, hyphenated) on FishView
Beware: - Some species have multiple tips (different specimens/vouchers). The image agent should provide ONE image per species — the R script will handle deduplication. - Some tip labels have underscores in unexpected places (e.g., Bodianus_rufus_F112 — the species is rufus, not rufus_F112). The regex above handles this.

title: Brownstein Labrid Tree — Handoff for FishView Image Agent
text:
For each of the 4 labrid family directories on FishView:
~/fishview-data/families/<family>/
├── images_fishwise/     ← FishWise lateral views (preferred)
├── images_fishbase/     ← FishBase photos
├── images_inaturalist/  ← iNaturalist CC photos
└── segmented/           ← alpha-masked PNGs (post-processing)
Minimum viable set for Demo 7 Part 2: 10 species per group, spanning genera. The tip label CSVs list every species in the tree — the agent should prioritize species that are: 1. In the tree (required) 2. Visually distinctive (good for color analysis) 3. Spread across genera (not all one genus)
